summary:
Shah Jahan's reign (1628–1658 AD) represented the cultural apogee of the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] — an era of architectural magnificence and courtly splendor that produced the Taj Mahal, the Red Fort of Delhi, and the Jama Masjid mosque, as well as a refinement of Mughal miniature painting, music, and poetry that contemporaries recognized as the pinnacle of Indo-Islamic civilization. Shah Jahan ('King of the World'), son of Jahangir and grandson of [[event:2381|Akbar]], inherited a prosperous empire and devoted enormous resources to construction — the [[event:2382|Taj Mahal]] (built 1632–1653) as a mausoleum for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth bearing their 14th child, consumed 32 million rupees and 20,000 workers over two decades.

Shah Jahan's military record was mixed — Mughal campaigns in Central Asia to recapture Samarkand, the ancestral homeland, repeatedly failed, and the empire's northwestern frontier was never secured. His reign ended in tragedy: in 1658, while Shah Jahan was ill, his son [[event:2507|Aurangzeb]] led a succession war against his brothers, imprisoned his father in Agra Fort, and seized the throne. Shah Jahan spent the last eight years of his life confined in the Agra Fort, reportedly able to see the Taj Mahal from his window — a poignant final chapter for the builder of the world's most celebrated monument to love. He died in 1666 and was buried in the Taj Mahal beside Mumtaz Mahal.
